original_hypothesis.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Deposit Creates Accounting Deficit Enabling Last-Withdrawer Loss
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The deposit() function credits balances[msg.sender][token] += amount without measuring the actual tokens received by the contract. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives less than amount but credits the full amount. Over multiple deposits, the cumulative accounting deficit grows. Later withdrawers find the contract insolvent—actual balance < total recorded balances—and cannot withdraw their funds.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The deposit function uses safeTransferFrom with the user-supplied amount and credits that exact amount without a before/after balance delta check. This is a well-known pattern but warrants inclusion because: (1) the contract is designed for multiple stablecoin types (TIP-20 is a standard, not a single token), and (2) some stablecoins may implement transfer fees or deductions.
deployment_assumptions: At least one supported TIP-20 token implements fee-on-transfer mechanics
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Contract accepts any IERC20 token address in deposit(). Documentation references 'pathUSD and other Tempo-native stablecoins' but no deployment script verifies token implementations. No transfer fee check exists in code.

proof_of_signal.pattern: deposit() credits balances[msg.sender][token] += amount after safeTransferFrom without measuring actual received via balanceOf delta

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is deposited into the DEX
Multiple deposits occur, accumulating the accounting deficit
Later withdrawers attempt to withdraw their full recorded balance

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: If token charges 1% transfer fee: deposit(100) → contract receives 99 but credits 100. After 100 deposits of 100: contract has 9900 tokens but credits total 10000. Last 1% of withdrawers lose 100% of their funds.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker deposits and immediately withdraws with fee-on-transfer token repeatedly, extracting the fee difference from the pool
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts transfer_fee_rate * deposit_amount per cycle from other depositors

economic_questions: Which TIP-20 stablecoins on Tempo chain have transfer fees?
What fee rates are typical for these tokens?
Is the deficit exploitable via rapid deposit/withdraw cycling?
validation_requirements: Identify specific TIP-20 tokens with transfer fee mechanics

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Deposit amount (returned minus fee each cycle)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: fee_rate * deposit_amount per cycle, extracted from other depositors
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on fee rate and number of cycles vs gas cost

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Capital for deposits; other depositors must exist
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When large deposits from other users exist
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Gradual insolvency; last withdrawers lose funds

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All depositors of the fee-on-transfer token (especially later withdrawers)
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% loss for last withdrawers
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative transfer fee deficit across all deposits

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check TIP-20 token specifications for transfer fee support
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model deficit accumulation over N deposits

validation_result.impact_summary: The deposit() function credits the full user-supplied `amount` without measuring actual tokens received via a balanceOf delta, creating a growing accounting deficit when fee-on-transfer tokens are used. This leads to contract insolvency where later withdrawers cannot retrieve their funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The deposit() function at StablecoinDEX.sol lines 137-147 performs safeTransferFrom for `amount` and then credits `balances[msg.sender][token] += amount` and `totalDeposits[token] += amount`. For a fee-on-transfer token that deducts X% on every transfer, the contract actually receives `amount - fee` but records the full `amount`. This creates an ever-growing deficit between the contract's actual token balance and the sum of all recorded user balances. The contract has no token whitelist—any IERC20 address is accepted—so this defect applies to any fee-on-transfer token deposited. The standard mitigation (measuring balanceOf before and after the transfer) is absent. This is a genuine code defect: the accounting logic is wrong for a class of ERC20 tokens that the contract does not filter out.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 137-147 of StablecoinDEX.sol clearly show the deposit function crediting the full `amount` parameter without any balance delta measurement. The withdraw function at lines 155-165 deducts from `balances[msg.sender][token]` and attempts `safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount)`. If the contract's actual token balance is less than the sum of all credited balances (due to accumulated transfer fees), the safeTransfer will revert for late withdrawers, causing loss of funds.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Credits full `amount` to user's balance without measuring actual tokens received. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives less than `amount` but records the full value, inflating internal accounting beyond actual holdings.

Attempts to transfer the full recorded balance to the user. When cumulative accounting deficit from fee-on-transfer deposits exceeds the contract's actual token holdings, safeTransfer reverts for later withdrawers even though their recorded balance appears sufficient.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls deposit(feeToken, 1000) where feeToken charges 1% transfer fee
2. safeTransferFrom transfers 1000 from user but contract receives only 990 (10 deducted as fee)
5. After 100 such deposits of 1000 each: contract holds 99000 tokens but records 100000
6. When users try to withdraw their full recorded balances, the last ~1000 tokens worth of withdrawals will fail because the contract only has 99000 tokens
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: deposit() at line 141 performs `IERC20(token).safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), amount)` which for a fee-on-transfer token delivers less than `amount`. Then line 142 `balances[msg.sender][token] += amount` credits the full requested amount. No balanceOf delta check exists anywhere between lines 141 and 142. The deficit accumulates across all deposits. When withdraw() at line 160 calls `IERC20(token).safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount)`, if the contract's actual token balance is less than the requested amount (due to accumulated deficits), the transfer reverts, locking legitimate depositors out of their funds.

deployment_assumptions_checked: At least one supported TIP-20 token implements fee-on-transfer mechanics
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that specify which tokens are supported. The foundry.toml only configures compiler and remappings. The contract itself accepts any arbitrary IERC20 address with no whitelist or token validation, meaning fee-on-transfer tokens can be deposited without restriction.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: (1) A fee-on-transfer token (1% fee) is used with the DEX. (2) Alice deposits 10000 tokens → contract receives 9900, credits 10000. (3) Bob deposits 10000 tokens → contract receives 9900, credits 10000. (4) Contract holds 19800 tokens, records 20000. (5) Alice withdraws 10000 → succeeds, contract now has 9800 tokens. (6) Bob tries to withdraw 10000 → safeTransfer reverts because contract only holds 9800. Bob loses 200 tokens. Active exploitation: An attacker can accelerate the deficit by rapidly depositing and withdrawing fee-on-transfer tokens, each cycle extracting the fee difference from the pool's overall solvency.
validation_result.impact_assessment: For any fee-on-transfer token deposited into the contract, the accounting invariant (contract balance >= sum of all user balances) is violated. The deficit grows linearly with deposits. Eventually, the contract becomes insolvent for that token, and late withdrawers lose funds proportional to the cumulative transfer fee deficit. This is a direct violation of INV-ECON-001 (physical solvency) and INV-ECON-002 (conservation).
recommended_mitigations: Measure actual tokens received using a before/after balanceOf delta pattern: `uint256 before = IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this)); safeTransferFrom(...); uint256 received = IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this)) - before; balances[msg.sender][token] += uint128(received);`
Alternatively, implement a token whitelist that only allows known non-fee-on-transfer tokens
Add explicit documentation and revert if the received amount differs from the requested amount to reject fee-on-transfer tokens entirely
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Create a MockFeeToken ERC20 that deducts 1% on every transfer. 2. Deploy StablecoinDEX. 3. Two users each deposit 10000 MockFeeTokens. 4. Verify contract balanceOf is 19800 but totalDeposits shows 20000. 5. First user withdraws 10000 successfully. 6. Second user attempts to withdraw 10000 — expect revert due to insufficient contract balance. 7. Verify the second user can only withdraw ~9800, losing ~200 tokens.
uncertainties: Whether any TIP-20 stablecoin on Tempo chain actually implements fee-on-transfer mechanics is unknown without deployment scripts or token specs
The exact fee rate of any such token is unknown, affecting the severity of the deficit accumulation rate
additional_testing_needed: Identify all TIP-20 tokens planned for deployment on Tempo chain and audit their transfer mechanics
Test with various fee rates (0.1%, 1%, 5%) to quantify insolvency timeline
Verify whether the rapid deposit/withdraw cycling attack is gas-profitable at various fee rates
